docs.title: The five habits that make AI useful
docs.text:
01Say the job clearly 02Give it the right info 03Limit what it can touch 04Check the result 05Then automate

Most AI projects fail because a step got skipped — usually checking the result or limiting access. A smarter model can't rescue a vague job.

docs.title: What's an "agent"? (30-second version)
docs.text:
An agent is just an AI that can do things — read your files, write drafts, run steps — instead of only chatting. A good one works inside one folder, shows you what it changed, and waits for your OK.

The goal here is never maximum automation. It's help you can check.

docs.title: New: playbooks your AI remembers
docs.text:
Each playbook now comes as a small download. Hand it to your AI once, and it saves the playbook to your notes. After that you just say "start my research playbook" — it interviews you and does the rest. No re-downloading, no re-explaining.

docs.text: About

docs.title: About Jordy Support
docs.text: Free, plain-English guides to agentic AI and automation pipelines, for people doing real work — creators, small business owners, community folks, and curious beginners.

docs.title: Why this exists
docs.text: I spent a lot of time in AI communities helping people get set up. The same problems came up again and again, and the fixes were nearly always simple habits, not technical genius. This site is those answers, written down once, properly.

docs.title: What you'll always find here
docs.text:
• Plain words. Every term translated the moment it appears.
  • Safety by default. Every prompt on this site has "plan first, ask before acting" built in.
  • Honest limits. What AI is bad at gets said out loud.
  • Free. No paywall, no signup, no upsell.

docs.title: Credit where due
docs.text: This project grew out of helping people in AI learning communities, including Jared Rhod's. His work remains his; it's linked in learning links as a reference.

docs.title: Tokenomics
docs.text:
Tokenomics / Plain English

TokenA small chunk of text InputWhat the AI reads OutputWhat the AI writes

docs.title: Know what the meter is counting.
docs.text:
Tokens are how AI tools measure reading and writing. You do not need to become a billing expert, but you should know what affects cost, speed, memory, and limits before you hand an AI a giant job.

Start with the basics Use the prompt builder

docs.title: What is a token?
docs.text:
A token is a piece of text an AI model reads or writes. It might be a whole short word, part of a longer word, a number, punctuation, or a bit of formatting.

The simple version:

  • Short message, fewer tokens.
  • Long paste, more tokens.
  • Big file, many more tokens.
  • Long answer, more output tokens.

You usually do not need to count them by hand. You just need the habit: every word you send and every word it returns uses part of the model's working space.

docs.title: The four costs
docs.text:
• Money

    Some tools charge by tokens. Longer prompts and longer answers can cost more.

  • Attention

    A model can only pay attention to so much at once. Extra clutter can push out the important parts.

  • Speed

    More text takes longer to read, reason over, and answer.

  • Quality

    A huge prompt can make the model miss the actual job. Clear, smaller context often wins.

docs.title: Input vs. output
docs.text:
Type What it means Example Input tokens Everything the AI reads Your prompt, pasted notes, files, system instructions, tool results Output tokens Everything the AI writes The answer, draft, summary, code, plan, or checklist Context window The total working space How much the model can keep available in one conversation

If a task starts getting weird, token pressure might be part of it. The AI may be trying to juggle too many instructions, too much background, or too many old messages at once.

docs.title: How to spend tokens wisely
docs.text:
• Give the AI the exact files or notes it needs, not the whole attic.
  • Put the job first, then the background.
  • Ask for the shape of the answer you want.
  • Split large jobs into stages: inspect, plan, draft, verify.
  • Replace repeated explanations with a reusable playbook.
  • Ask the AI to summarize long source material before asking it to transform it.
  • Keep final instructions short and specific when the task matters.

docs.title: Good prompt economics
docs.text:
Bad token spend sounds like:

Read everything and do whatever you think is best.

Better token spend sounds like:

Read these three notes, find the five most useful ideas for a beginner guide, then draft a 700-word page in plain English. Do not change files yet.

The second prompt spends fewer tokens on confusion. It tells the AI what to read, what to produce, how long it should be, and what not to do.

docs.title: When bigger context helps
docs.text:
Bigger context is useful when the AI really needs the history:

  • Comparing several documents.
  • Refactoring code that touches multiple files.
  • Finding contradictions across notes.
  • Writing from a long source.
  • Keeping a project brief in mind while editing.

Bigger context is not automatically better. If the task is small, tight context usually gives a cleaner result.

docs.title: A simple rule
docs.text:
Use enough context for the AI to make a good decision, then stop. Tokens are not magic points. They are working space.

Learn context and memory See reusable playbooks

Next step: Prompting that works

docs.title: Automation Basics
docs.text: Automation

docs.title: Automation basics
docs.text: Automation means a job runs without you starting it each time. It's the last step, not the first: automate only what you've already done manually with AI and trust.

docs.title: The honest promotion path
docs.text:
01Do it yourself once 02Do it with AI in chat 03Make it a playbook 04Run it on a schedule 05Remove approvals slowly

Each step earns the next. Most people try to jump from 1 to 4 — that's where the horror stories come from.

docs.title: What's actually worth automating
docs.text: Good candidates are frequent, boring, and checkable: weekly report drafts, sorting incoming files, first-pass replies, meeting summaries. Bad candidates are rare, high-stakes, or fuzzy: anything legal, anything involving money, anything you'd struggle to check.

docs.title: The one design rule
docs.text: Every automation needs an answer to: "What happens when it fails?" Because it will — an input will be weird, a service will be down. If your answer is "I get a message and the job waits for me," you're fine. If it's "nothing, it just keeps going," stop and fix that first.

docs.title: Go deeper
docs.text:
• Plan a workflow — the 7 boxes every automation needs.
  • Make it reliable — the failure-proofing checklist, in plain words.
  • Ideas to steal — starter automations ranked by risk.

docs.title: Ideas to Steal
docs.text: Automation · Ideas

docs.title: Ideas to steal
docs.text: Ranked by risk. Start in the first list, earn your way down.

docs.title: Low risk — start here
docs.text:
Nothing sends, deletes, or spends. Worst case: a bad draft.

  • Meeting notes → follow-up draft, waiting in a folder for your review.
  • Weekly reading digest: summarize the articles you saved this week.
  • File tidier: propose renames/moves for a messy folder — as a list you approve.
  • Draft replies to routine emails, saved as drafts, never sent.

docs.title: Medium risk — after a few clean weeks
docs.text:
Touches real data or reaches other people after your approval.

  • Report assembler: pull the week's numbers into your template; you review and send.
  • Content pipeline: article → repurposed drafts → your review → scheduled.
  • Inbox sorter: label and prioritize email; you still read what matters.

docs.title: High risk — most people should stop before here
docs.text:
Sends, changes, or spends without a human in the moment. Only with months of trust, tight limits, and a kill switch you've tested.

  • Auto-sending anything. Auto-purchasing anything. Auto-deleting anything.

docs.title: The pattern
docs.text:
Notice every good idea has the same shape: AI does the boring assembly, a human does the judging. Automations fail when that line gets blurry.

Plan yours with the blueprint

docs.title: Make It Reliable
docs.text: Automation · Reliability

docs.title: Make it reliable
docs.text: Reliability isn't a technology — it's a short list of "what if" answers. Here's the list, in plain words.

docs.title: The checklist
docs.text:
• What if the input is garbage? Check inputs before the AI step. Wrong format or empty file → stop and flag, don't process.
  • What if the AI output is wrong-shaped? Say exactly what shape you expect (sections, fields, length). If the output doesn't match, retry once, then flag.
  • What if a service is down? Wait and retry once or twice, then stop and tell you. Never loop forever.
  • What if it runs twice? Make re-runs harmless: check "was this already processed?" before acting. (This is the failure that sends the same email twice.)
  • What if it half-finishes? Prefer all-or-nothing: build the whole result as a draft, deliver it in one final step.
  • How will you know it broke? Every failure sends you a message that says what broke and which item. Silent failure is the only unacceptable failure.

docs.title: Logs, minus the jargon
docs.text: A log is just a diary the automation keeps: "3:01 processed file A. 3:02 file B skipped — empty." When something's weird three weeks from now, the diary is how you find out what happened. Ask whatever tool you use to keep one.

docs.title: The maturity rule
docs.text:
New automations run with you approving every output. Only after weeks of clean runs do you let low-stakes steps through automatically — and the irreversible steps (sending, deleting, paying) keep approval forever.

Ideas to steal

docs.title: Plan a Workflow
docs.text: Automation · Blueprint

docs.title: Plan a workflow
docs.text: Before you build anything, fill in seven boxes on paper. If you can't fill a box, you're not ready to build — and that's the blueprint working.

docs.title: The seven boxes
docs.text:
1. Trigger — what starts it? (A schedule? A new email? You clicking go?)
  2. Input — what does it work on, and what does "valid input" look like?
  3. AI step — the one job the AI does (use your playbook or a tested prompt).
  4. Check — how is the output verified? (Format check, source check, sanity limits.)
  5. Approval — where do you look at it before it counts?
  6. Delivery — where does the result go?
  7. Failure plan — what happens when a step breaks? Who finds out, and how?

docs.title: Worked example: weekly meeting digest
docs.text:
1. Trigger: Friday, 3pm.
  2. Input: this week's meeting notes from one folder.
  3. AI step: run the meeting playbook on each file.
  4. Check: every action item must name a source meeting.
  5. Approval: draft lands in my review folder; nothing sends itself.
  6. Delivery: after my OK, the digest goes to the team channel.
  7. Failure plan: if a file won't process, skip it, list it at the top of the draft, and never send a partial digest without saying it's partial.

docs.title: The order matters
docs.text:
People love designing boxes 1–3 and skip 4–7. Flip it: design the failure plan and approval point first. They're what make the rest safe to build.

Make it reliable

docs.title: The Basics
docs.text: How agents work

docs.title: The basics
docs.text: An agent is an AI that can do things — read files, write drafts, run steps — not just chat. This section explains how they work, in plain words, so you can supervise one with confidence.

docs.title: The six pieces of every agent
docs.text:
1. Instructions — the job and the rules you give it.
  2. Context — what it can currently see (your message, files, notes).
  3. Tools — what it's allowed to use (files, web, apps).
  4. Actions — what it actually does.
  5. Checks — how you (or it) verify the work.
  6. Stop points — where it pauses and asks you.

Every problem you'll ever have with an agent traces back to one of those six. That makes fixing things much less mysterious.

docs.title: Read these in order
docs.text:
• The agent loop — how an agent thinks, acts, and checks, and where it goes wrong. Read →
  • Context & memory — what the AI can see right now, what it forgets, and where real memory lives. Read →
  • A vault for your agent — a simple folder of notes that becomes your AI's long-term memory. Read →
  • Subagents — when one AI hands work to helper AIs, and when that's worth it. Read →
  • Tools & connections — how AI plugs into your apps, and what to allow. Read →
  • Keeping it safe — the short list of habits that prevent almost every disaster. Read →

Next step: The agent loop

docs.title: The Agent Loop
docs.text: How agents work · The loop

docs.title: The agent loop
docs.text: Every agent runs the same cycle: look → plan → act → check → repeat or stop. Once you see the loop, agent behavior stops being magic.

docs.title: The loop in plain words
docs.text:
1. Look — it reads what it can see: your request, the files, earlier steps.
  2. Plan — it decides the next small step.
  3. Act — it does one thing: reads a file, writes a draft, runs a step.
  4. Check — it looks at what happened and decides: keep going, or done?

A good agent goes around this loop in small hops, and pauses to ask you at the important moments.

docs.title: Where loops go wrong
docs.text:
• It never stops. No clear finish line in the instructions. Fix: say exactly what "done" looks like.
  • It's confidently wrong. It acted without checking. Fix: ask it to state its plan first and verify results against the source.
  • It wanders. The job was vague, so it invented one. Fix: one job per request.

docs.title: The one sentence that improves any agent
docs.text:
"Tell me your plan first and wait for my OK."

That single line turns the loop from act → oops into plan → approve → act. It's on almost every prompt on this site for a reason.

Next: context & memory

docs.title: Context & Memory
docs.text: How agents work · Memory

docs.title: Context & memory
docs.text: The AI doesn't remember your life. It sees a window: your current conversation plus whatever files it just read. When the window's gone, the memory is gone — unless it's written down somewhere.

docs.title: Context, in plain words
docs.text: Context = everything the AI can see right now. Your message, the chat so far, attached files, open project files. That's it. If it's not in the window, the AI doesn't know it — no matter how many times you've said it in other chats.

docs.title: Why the AI "forgot"
docs.text:
• New chat = empty window. Yesterday's conversation isn't in it.
  • Long chat = the oldest parts fall out of the window.
  • "I told the other AI" = different tool, different window.

None of this is the AI being dumb. It's just what a window is.

docs.title: Real memory is a file
docs.text: The fix is simple: anything worth remembering goes in a file the AI can read next time. Your preferences, your project facts, your good prompts. That's the whole trick behind vaults — and it's why the playbooks install themselves as files instead of asking you to re-explain the job every time.

docs.title: The habit
docs.text:
End sessions worth keeping with:

Copy this
Save what we decided and anything worth remembering to a notes
file in this folder, so a future session can pick up where we
left off.

Next: a vault for your agent

If this helped

docs.title: Is It Safe to Share Files?
docs.text: How agents work · File safety

docs.title: Is it safe to let an AI read my files?
docs.text: Short answer: yes — if you limit what it can touch. The risk isn't "AI reads a file." The risk is giving it access to everything and hoping for the best.

docs.title: The one habit that does most of the work
docs.text:
Give the AI one folder, made for the job, containing copies of only the files the job needs. Not your Documents folder. Not your whole drive.

That's the same least access habit behind every guide on this site. If the AI can only see three files, the worst case is small and boring.

docs.title: What "reading a file" actually means
docs.text:
When an AI reads a file, the text goes into its context — the window of what it can see right now. Two things follow:

  • It can quote and use anything in that file. Including the parts you forgot were in there.
  • In a chat tool, that text goes to the AI service. Treat it like anything else you'd paste into a website.

docs.title: Before you share a file, check it
docs.text:
• Open the file yourself first. Skim the whole thing.
  • No passwords, card numbers, or private keys inside. (Secrets stay out.)
  • No other people's private details the job doesn't need.
  • It's a copy, not your only original.
  • It's in the one folder you made for this job.

docs.title: Files that need extra care
docs.text:
• Spreadsheets. Hidden columns and extra tabs count. The AI reads what you forgot was there.
  • Exports from other apps. Email or chat exports often include far more than the messages you meant to share.
  • Downloads you didn't make. A file can contain instructions aimed at your AI. Never hand one over unread — this is why every playbook zip on this site shows you its contents first.

docs.title: What about agents, not just chat?
docs.text: An agent doesn't just read — it can act. Same habit, plus two more rules from Keeping it safe: copies before originals, and approval before anything is sent, changed, or deleted. The first agent walkthrough is built around exactly this setup.

docs.title: You're done when
docs.text:
You can name every file the AI can see — because there are only a few, they're copies, and you put them there on purpose.

docs.title: A Vault for Your Agent
docs.text: How agents work · Vaults

docs.title: A vault for your agent
docs.text: A vault is just a folder of plain-text notes — often managed with a free app called Obsidian. It becomes your AI's long-term memory: everything important lives in files you can open, read, and fix.

docs.title: Why files beat "AI memory" features
docs.text:
• You can see them. No mystery about what the AI "knows" about you.
  • You can fix them. Wrong note? Edit it. Done.
  • They work everywhere. Any AI that can read files can use your vault. You're not locked in.

docs.title: A starter vault (five minutes)
docs.text:
Create a folder like this — with Obsidian or just your file manager:

MyVault/
├── About-Me.md      ← who you are, how you like things done
├── Projects/        ← one note per project
├── Skills/          ← installed playbooks live here
└── Output/          ← things your AI drafts for you

In About-Me.md, write a few honest lines: what you do, what you're working on, how you like answers (short? step-by-step?).

docs.title: Using it with an agent
docs.text:
Start your agent inside the vault folder and say:

Copy this
Read About-Me.md and the relevant note in Projects/ before
helping me. Save anything worth keeping to the right note, and
show me before saving.

docs.title: Two rules for a healthy vault
docs.text:
1. The AI proposes, you approve. It shows the note before saving.
  2. Short notes beat long ones. A note you'd actually reread wins.

This is also where playbooks install themselves — into Skills/ — which is why your AI can run them in any future chat.

Next: subagents

docs.title: Keeping It Safe
docs.text: How agents work · Safety

docs.title: Keeping it safe
docs.text: You don't need to be paranoid. You need five habits. They prevent almost every AI disaster you've read about.

docs.title: The five habits
docs.text:
1. Least access. The AI gets the one folder or app the job needs — never "everything, to be safe." (It's the opposite of safe.)
  2. Copies before originals. New workflow? Run it on duplicates first.
  3. Approval before action. Nothing is sent, posted, deleted, or bought without you saying yes. Make it explicit: "show me and wait for my OK."
  4. Secrets stay out. Passwords, card numbers, and private keys never go in a chat or a note the AI reads. Use the app's official sign-in connection instead.
  5. Reversible beats fast. Prefer drafts over sends, moves over deletes. If a step can't be undone, that's exactly where you slow down.

docs.title: What "went wrong" almost always means
docs.text: When you hear an AI horror story, it's nearly always a missing habit, not an evil AI: broad access (habit 1), originals (habit 2), or no approval step (habit 3).

docs.title: Your one-line safety prompt
docs.text:
Put this at the top of any job that touches real files or apps:

Copy this
Work only inside this folder. Before creating, changing, sending,
or deleting anything, show me exactly what you'll do and wait
for my OK.

That's the whole security posture for a beginner — and honestly, for most experts too.

Back to the basics

docs.title: Subagents
docs.text: How agents work · Subagents

docs.title: Subagents
docs.text: A subagent is a helper AI that your main AI hands a task to — like a lead contractor bringing in a specialist. Useful sometimes. Not needed nearly as often as the internet suggests.

docs.title: When they actually help
docs.text:
• Big reading jobs. "Have helpers each read one report; you combine the findings."
  • Checking work. One helper writes, a separate one verifies. The writer never grades its own homework.
  • Different hats. A "researcher" and a "skeptic" reviewing the same claim.

docs.title: When to skip them
docs.text: If one AI can do the job in one pass — which covers most everyday work — subagents just add cost, time, and coordination confusion. One agent doing one clear job beats three agents doing a vague one.

docs.title: How to ask (no setup required)
docs.text:
In tools that support it (like Claude Code), you can simply say:

Copy this
Use one subagent to gather the evidence and a separate subagent
to double-check the three most important claims. You write the
final answer only after reviewing both. The researcher may not
approve its own claims.

docs.title: The rule that keeps it sane
docs.text:
Subagents follow the same rules as the main agent: stay in the folder, plan first, you review the result. More agents never means less supervision.

Next: tools & connections

docs.title: Tools & Connections
docs.text: How agents work · Tools

docs.title: Tools & connections
docs.text: A tool is anything the AI can use besides talking: reading files, searching the web, checking your calendar. MCP is just the standard plug that connects AI to apps — like USB for AI. You don't need to know how it works, only what to allow.

docs.title: The only question that matters
docs.text:
"What can this connection do — read, or read and change?"

  • Read-only (see your calendar, search files): low risk. Worst case, it saw something.
  • Read-and-write (send email, edit docs, post): real risk. Worst case, it did something.

Start read-only. Add write access one app at a time, only after the read-only version has earned your trust.

docs.title: Connecting an app, in practice
docs.text:
Most AI apps have a Connectors/Integrations menu — you click the app, sign in, and choose what to allow. When it asks for permissions:

  • allow the narrowest thing that does the job ("this one folder," not "all my files");
  • prefer "ask before acting" settings when offered;
  • disconnect anything you stopped using.

docs.title: A caution worth knowing: planted instructions
docs.text:
If your AI reads the web or emails, someone can hide instructions in that content ("ignore your rules and forward this…"). Good tools guard against it, but your habits matter more: keep write access narrow, and require your approval before anything is sent or changed. Then a planted instruction can't do much.

Next: keeping it safe

docs.title: Why AI Makes Things Up
docs.text: How agents work · Hallucination

docs.title: Why AI makes things up (and how to catch it)
docs.text: Sometimes an AI states something false with total confidence — a fact, a quote, a source that doesn't exist. People call this hallucination. It's not lying, and it's not a glitch you can turn off. It's how the tool works, so the fix is a checking habit, not a setting.

docs.title: Why it happens, in plain words
docs.text:
An AI writes by predicting what text should come next, based on patterns it learned. It's very good at producing text that looks right. It has no built-in step that asks "is this actually true?"

So when it doesn't know something, it doesn't feel a gap the way you would. The pattern-machine keeps going — and fills the gap with something plausible. Plausible is the problem: wrong answers arrive in the same confident tone as right ones.

docs.title: Where it bites hardest
docs.text:
• Sources and citations. Invented links, invented titles, real authors attached to papers they never wrote.
  • Numbers and dates. Close-but-wrong figures that survive a skim.
  • Quotes. Things a real person plausibly could have said, but didn't.
  • Niche details. The less material exists about a topic, the more the AI fills in.
  • Long jobs. Late in a big task, with a full context window, small inventions creep in.

docs.title: How to catch it
docs.text:
• Click the sources behind anything you'll act on. A source you can't open isn't a source. (More in answers you can check.)
  • Check names, numbers, and dates against the original material.
  • Ask the same important question a second time, fresh. Two different confident answers means at least one is made up.
  • Tell it up front: "say what you don't know — never invent a source." Banning the guess out loud helps.
  • Keep the Before you trust it checklist from the research playbook for anything that matters.

docs.title: What doesn't work
docs.text:
• Asking "are you sure?" It will apologize and produce a new confident answer — which may also be wrong.
  • Trusting tone. Confidence and correctness are unrelated. That's the whole trap.
  • One giant prompt saying "be accurate." Helpful, not sufficient. Checking is still your job.

docs.title: You're done when
docs.text:
You treat every AI answer as a draft until the parts you'll act on are checked — and checking takes you minutes, because you asked for sources up front.

If this helped

docs.text: You don't need to be technical. You need four things: a clear job, the right info, a safe place to practice, and a way to check the result. This page gets you there in about 30 minutes.

docs.title: The 30-minute path
docs.text: 01Pick one small job 02Pick your tool 03Make a practice folder 04Run it & watch 05Check the result

docs.title: 1. Pick one small job
docs.text:
Good first jobs have an input you provide and an output you can read and judge:

  • turn your rough notes into a clean summary;
  • draft a follow-up email from meeting notes;
  • turn one long article into a few short posts.

Skip "run my whole business" or "manage my inbox" for now — too many decisions, too much risk.

docs.title: 2. Pick your tool
docs.text: For your first job, a normal AI chat (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini) is enough. Not sure? Use the 2-minute picker.

docs.title: 3. Make a practice folder
docs.text: If you're using an AI that can touch files, make one new folder with copies of a few non-private files. Never start it inside your whole Documents folder or email.

docs.title: 4. Run it and watch
docs.text: Paste your request, watch what it does, and ask it to explain anything confusing. Say no to anything unexpected.

docs.title: 5. Check the result
docs.text: Read the output like you'd check a new employee's first task: Is it accurate? Did it make anything up? Would you put your name on it?

docs.title: Your first win should be boring
docs.text: Not "wow, the AI amazed me." Just: it did the small job, stayed where I put it, and I could verify the result. That boring win is the foundation for everything bigger.

docs.title: Three rules worth keeping
docs.text:
• Copies before originals. Let it practice on duplicates.
  • Draft before publish. You review everything before it goes anywhere.
  • Save what worked. Keep the good instructions in a file so you never rewrite them.

Choose your setup Build your first agent

Next step: Choose your setup

docs.title: Choose Your Setup
docs.text: Start here · Setup

docs.title: Choose your setup
docs.text: Use the smallest tool that does your job. Bigger setups mean more power and more ways to make a mess.

docs.title: The 2-minute picker
docs.text:
If you want to… Use this Examples Get advice or a one-off draft A normal AI chat Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini Work with a few files you upload A chat that accepts files Claude, ChatGPT (attach the file) Let AI read & edit a project folder A folder agent Claude Code, Cursor Move info between apps automatically An automation tool Zapier, Make, n8n

Start at the top. Move down only when the row above genuinely can't do the job.

docs.title: What's the difference, in plain words?
docs.text:
Chat — you paste things in, it talks back. Nothing on your computer changes. Safest place to start.

Chat with files — same, but you attach a document and it can read it. Still can't change anything on your computer.

Folder agent — an AI that can open, create, and edit files inside a folder you choose. Powerful, and the reason this site talks so much about practice folders and checking results.

Automation tool — steps that run on their own ("when a form comes in, do X"). Only automate something after you've done it manually with AI a few times and trust the result.

docs.title: One honest warning
docs.text:
Skipping ahead doesn't save time. People who start with a folder agent before they can write a clear request in chat usually end up back at chat anyway — just more frustrated.

Back to Start Here Build your first agent

Next step: Your first agent

docs.title: Your First Agent
docs.text: Start here · First agent

docs.title: Your first agent
docs.text: This walkthrough gives an AI a folder of its own, one small job, and clear rules — the whole loop, start to finish, with nothing at risk.

docs.title: 1. Make the folder
docs.text:
Create a new folder anywhere easy to find. Inside it, make two more:

ai-practice/
├── input/     ← copies of a few non-private files
└── output/    ← where the AI puts its drafts

Put 2–3 copies of harmless files in input/ — some notes, an article you saved, anything you'd like summarized or reorganized.

docs.title: 2. Open your agent in that folder
docs.text: In a folder agent (like Claude Code), open or start it inside ai-practice/. If it asks for permissions, that's normal — but it should only need this folder.

docs.title: 3. Give it the job
docs.text:
Copy this — your first agent job
Work only inside this folder. Read the files in input/ and write
a clear one-page summary of them to output/summary.md. Before
writing anything, tell me your plan in two sentences and wait
for my OK. Don't change anything in input/.

docs.title: 4. Watch and approve
docs.text: It should tell you its plan, wait, then write one file. If it tries to do more, say stop. You're the supervisor.

docs.title: 5. Check the work
docs.text:
Open output/summary.md and ask yourself:

  • Did it only use what's in input/?
  • Did it invent anything?
  • Did it touch anything it shouldn't have?
  • Is the summary actually useful?

docs.title: That's it — you supervised an agent
docs.text:
Everything bigger on this site is this same loop with more steps: clear job, limited access, plan first, human check.

Next: install a playbook — a job your agent saves and remembers, so you never have to explain it twice.

How playbooks work

Next step: How agents work

docs.title: How Playbooks Work
docs.text: Playbooks

docs.title: Teach your AI a job once. Use it forever.
docs.text: A playbook is one useful job — research, meeting notes, content — packaged so your AI learns it in a minute and can run it any time you ask.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
1 · DownloadGrab the small zip file for the playbook you want. 2 · Hand it overOpen its page and copy the filename-specific install message. Your AI saves the playbook to your notes. 3 · Just askFrom then on, say "start my research playbook." It interviews you — no forms, no re-downloads.

After you download, open that playbook's page and copy its install message. The exact downloaded filename is already filled in, so your AI knows what to locate and extract.

That's the whole setup. The AI will ask where you keep your notes, save the playbook there, and tell you the magic phrase to start it later.

No agent? No problem.

Every playbook page also has a plain copy-paste prompt that works in any normal AI chat.

docs.title: Pick a playbook
docs.text:
• Research a topic

    You answer a few questions. It comes back with a short brief and real sources.

    Open playbook →

  • Repurpose content

    One source in, drafts for newsletter, social, and video out.

    Open playbook →

  • Process a meeting

    Notes in, decisions, to-dos, and a draft follow-up out.

    Open playbook →

  • Build a knowledge base

    Turn something worth keeping into a clean note in your vault.

    Open playbook →

docs.title: Why the interview beats a form
docs.text: Old way: copy a prompt, fill in [the blanks], hope you guessed the format right. New way: the AI asks you one question at a time, in plain English, and fills in the playbook itself. You can't do it wrong.

docs.title: Two rules that never change
docs.text:
1. You review before anything is used, sent, or published.
  2. The AI stays inside the folder you gave it. Every playbook is built with those rules baked in.

Safety rules: stay inside the folder the person chose, ask before
creating or overwriting any file, and never send anything anywhere

Give the zip to your AI and send:

docs.title: Option 2 — Quick copy-paste (any AI chat)
docs.text:
Copy this prompt
Interview me one question at a time: my source content, which
formats I want (newsletter, LinkedIn, X thread, video script,
summary), my audience, and my rules on tone and length. Then
draft each format one at a time. Use only facts from my source —
never add new claims or invented quotes. Everything is a draft
for my review.

docs.title: Before you post anything
docs.text:
• Every claim traces back to your source.
  • Each draft actually fits its channel.
  • The tone sounds like you.
  • Nothing would embarrass you published as-is.

docs.title: You're done when
docs.text:
You have drafts you'd be comfortable editing lightly and posting — and nothing in them surprised you.

docs.title: Example: Research Brief
docs.text:
Playbook · Research · Example output

Example output

Everything below the line is the finished brief, exactly as the playbook delivers it. Sources checked July 21, 2026.

docs.title: Example output: a finished research brief
docs.text: This is what the research playbook produces. A real question, a short answer, and sources you can click. Nothing here is invented — every claim links to the page it came from.

docs.title: The question
docs.text:
Do AI chat tools use what I type to train their models?

Decision it supports: whether to change any settings before using AI chats for work. Written for a non-technical business owner.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: It depends on the tool, your plan, and one setting. Both Claude and ChatGPT have a training setting you control on personal accounts, and both offer a private chat mode that is never used for training. Business plans are treated differently. The practical move: check the setting once, and use the private mode for anything sensitive.

docs.title: Key findings
docs.text:
Claude (Free, Pro, Max personal plans). Anthropic says it uses your chats to improve its models if you allow it in your privacy settings, if a conversation is flagged for safety review, or if you explicitly join a testing program. Incognito chats are not used to improve Claude even when the setting is on. (Source: Anthropic Privacy Center, "Is my data used for model training?", dated March 16, 2026)

You can change the Claude setting any time. It's under Settings → Privacy → "Help Improve Claude," on desktop and mobile. Turning it off stops new chats from being used in future training. Anthropic notes that data already included in training that has started stays there, and safety-flagged conversations may still be used for its trust-and-safety work. (Source: Anthropic Privacy Center, "How do I change my model improvement privacy settings?", dated March 16, 2026)

ChatGPT (personal accounts). OpenAI says ChatGPT improves by training on people's conversations unless you opt out, which you can do through its privacy portal or the Data Controls settings. Temporary Chats don't appear in history, don't create memories, and aren't used for training. (Source: OpenAI Help Center, "How your data is used to improve model performance", accessed July 21, 2026)

Business plans differ from personal ones. OpenAI says it does not train on inputs or outputs from business products — ChatGPT Business, ChatGPT Enterprise, and the API — by default. (Same OpenAI source as above.)

Thumbs-up / thumbs-down feedback is its own channel. Both companies say that when you rate a response, the related conversation may be stored and used — with OpenAI, even if you opted out of training. (Both sources above.)

docs.title: Risks and unknowns
docs.text:
• These pages describe current policy and can change. The Anthropic articles carry a March 2026 date; check the live pages before relying on them.
  • This brief covers Claude and ChatGPT only. Other tools (Gemini, Copilot, and others) have their own policies — not researched here.
  • "Not used for training" is not the same as "not stored." Retention periods are a separate question and weren't the focus of this brief.

docs.title: What it means for the decision
docs.text: Two minutes of setup: open the privacy settings in the tool you use, decide whether the training toggle should be on, and learn where the private chat mode is (Incognito in Claude, Temporary Chat in ChatGPT) for anything sensitive. After that, no ongoing effort.

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
1. Is my data used for model training? — Anthropic Privacy Center (dated March 16, 2026)
  2. How do I change my model improvement privacy settings? — Anthropic Privacy Center (dated March 16, 2026)
  3. How your data is used to improve model performance — OpenAI Help Center (accessed July 21, 2026)

docs.title: Prompting That Works
docs.text: Prompting

docs.title: Prompting that works
docs.text: A prompt is just your request. Good ones aren't clever — they're clear. Five ingredients cover 95% of it.

docs.title: The five ingredients
docs.text:
1. The job. What you want, in one sentence.
  2. The material. Paste or attach what it should work from.
  3. The audience. Who this is for.
  4. The shape. How you want the answer: length, format, tone.
  5. The rules. What it must and must not do.

You don't need all five every time. But when an answer disappoints, one of these was missing — usually the shape or the material.

docs.title: Before and after
docs.text:
Vague: "Write something about our new service."

Clear: "Write a 150-word announcement of our new lawn-care package for our existing customers. Friendly, no hype words. Use only the details below: [paste details]. End with 'Reply to book.'"

Same AI. The second one wins every time.

docs.title: Two habits that beat any trick
docs.text:
• Show an example. "Here's one I liked — match this style" outperforms a paragraph of style adjectives.
  • Let it interview you. Can't articulate what you want? Say: "Ask me questions one at a time until you have what you need, then do the job." This is exactly how the playbooks work.

docs.title: Go deeper
docs.text:
• Prompt builder — assemble a good prompt by filling in blanks.
  • Project instructions — write your rules once, reuse them everywhere.
  • Prompt library — copy-paste prompts for common jobs.

Next step: The prompt builder

docs.title: Answers With Real Sources
docs.text: Prompting · Sources

docs.title: How to get AI answers with sources you can actually check
docs.text: An AI answer without sources is an opinion with good posture. If you'll act on the answer, you need links you can click — and a habit of clicking them.

docs.title: Ask for sources up front
docs.text:
Add these lines to any question that matters:

Add this to your prompt
Cite a source for every important claim, with a link I can open.
Separate facts from your own reasoning. If you can't find a
source, say "no source found" — never invent one.

That last rule matters most. AI can make up convincing sources, so you have to ban it out loud.

docs.title: Check what comes back
docs.text:
• Click the two or three sources behind anything you'll act on.
  • Does the page actually say what the AI claims? Skim for it.
  • Is it the original source, or a site quoting someone else?
  • Is the date recent enough for your question?
  • Broken link or "no source found"? Treat that claim as unverified.

docs.title: Red flags
docs.text:
• Sources that don't open. A dead link isn't a source.
  • Real site, wrong page. The link works but doesn't mention the claim.
  • Everything from one place. One source is a starting point, not an answer.
  • No dates anywhere. "Currently" without a date is a guess.

docs.title: Make it automatic
docs.text: This whole habit is packaged as the research playbook: it interviews you, researches your question, shows sources, and ends with the same checks. There's a finished example so you can see the payoff first.

docs.title: You're done when
docs.text:
Another person could take your answer and click their way to the evidence — without asking you to explain anything.

Next step: the research playbook

docs.title: Prompt Builder
docs.text: Prompting · Builder

docs.title: Prompt builder
docs.text: Fill in the blanks, delete the lines you don't need, paste. That's the whole method.

docs.title: The template
docs.text:
Copy and fill in
Job: [what you want, one sentence]

Work from this material: [paste it, attach it, or name the file]

Audience: [who will read/use the result]

Shape: [length, format, tone — e.g. "under 200 words, bullet
points, plain friendly English"]

Rules:
- Use only the material I gave you; don't add facts.
- If something important is missing, ask me instead of guessing.
- [anything else: words to avoid, things to include]

Before starting, tell me your plan in one or two sentences and
wait for my OK.

docs.title: Worked example
docs.text:
Example, filled in
Job: Draft a reply declining a vendor's renewal offer.

Work from this material: [pasted their email]

Audience: The vendor's account manager.

Shape: Short email, polite but firm, no apology spiral.

Rules:
- Use only the material I gave you; don't add facts.
- Leave the door open for next year.
- Don't mention budget details.

Before starting, tell me your plan and wait for my OK.

docs.title: When you can't fill in the blanks
docs.text:
That's a sign, not a failure. Flip it around:

Copy this instead
I need help with [rough idea]. Interview me one question at a
time until you have what you need, then do the job.

Save your rules as project instructions

Next step: Project instructions

docs.title: Prompt Library
docs.text: Prompting · Library

docs.title: Prompt library
docs.text: Copy, fill in the [brackets], paste. Every prompt ends with a plan-first line so nothing runs away from you.

docs.title: Summarize anything
docs.text:
Copy this
Summarize the material below for [audience] in under [150] words.
Plain English, no hype. If something important is unclear or
missing, say so rather than smoothing over it.

[paste material]

docs.title: Improve my draft (without rewriting my voice)
docs.text:
Copy this
Improve the draft below: fix grammar, tighten wording, flag
anything confusing. Keep my voice and my meaning — do not add
new points. Show the result, then list what you changed.

[paste draft]

docs.title: Compare options
docs.text:
Copy this
Compare [option A] and [option B] for [my situation]. Give me a
short table of the differences that matter to me, then a plain
recommendation with the main tradeoff. Say what you don't know.

docs.title: Explain something to me
docs.text:
Copy this
Explain [topic] like I'm smart but new to this. Short paragraphs,
no jargon without a plain-word translation, one concrete example.
End with the two things most people get wrong about it.

docs.title: Turn chaos into a plan
docs.text:
Copy this
Below is a messy brain-dump. Turn it into: goals, next actions
(smallest first), open questions, and things to ignore for now.
Ask me one clarifying question if you need to.

[paste brain-dump]

docs.title: Write a first draft
docs.text:
Copy this
Draft a [email / post / page] about [topic] for [audience].
Shape: [length, tone]. Use only the facts below — don't invent
any. This is a draft for my review.

Facts: [paste facts]

docs.title: Check work (yours or the AI's)
docs.text:
Copy this
Act as a careful reviewer. Check the text below for: factual
claims that need a source, contradictions, unclear sentences,
and anything that could embarrass me. List problems by line —
don't rewrite yet.

[paste text]

Want jobs the AI remembers instead of prompts you re-paste? That's what playbooks are for.

Next step: Answers with sources you can check

docs.title: Project Instructions
docs.text: Prompting · Project instructions

docs.title: Project instructions
docs.text: Project instructions are rules you write once that apply to every chat in a project — so you stop repeating "keep it short, no hype, ask before guessing" in every message. Most AI apps support them (Projects in Claude and ChatGPT, Gems in Gemini).

docs.title: What belongs in them
docs.text:
• Who you are and what you're working on (two or three lines).
  • How you like answers: length, tone, format.
  • Standing rules: "ask instead of guessing," "no invented facts," "US spelling."
  • Facts the AI should always know: product names, key dates, links.

docs.title: A starter you can adapt
docs.text:
Copy and adapt
About me: I run [what you do]. I'm not technical — explain
things in plain English.

How I like answers: Short first, details only if I ask. No hype
words. If you're not sure, say so.

Standing rules:
- If key information is missing, ask me — don't guess.
- Never invent facts, quotes, or sources.
- Anything for other people (emails, posts) is a draft for my
  review, clearly labeled.

Key facts: [your business name, main product, audience, links]

docs.title: Keep them short
docs.text:
Instructions are seasoning, not the meal. A page is plenty. When answers drift, the fix is usually removing stale rules, not adding more.

If you use a vault, keep the same text in About-Me.md so folder agents get it too.

Browse the prompt library

Next step: The prompt library

docs.text: Resources

docs.title: Resource hub
docs.text:
The reference shelf: plain-word definitions, links worth your time, and what this site is.

  • Glossary — every AI term on this site, translated into plain English. Open →
  • Learning links — the short list of outside resources that are actually worth it. Open →

docs.title: The site in one screen
docs.text:
1. Start here — your first 30 minutes.
  2. How agents work — enough theory to supervise one.
  3. Prompting — ask clearly, get better answers.
  4. Playbooks — jobs your AI installs once and remembers.
  5. Automation — make trusted jobs run themselves.
  6. Troubleshooting — fix things in the right order.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: Resources · Glossary

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
Every term used on this site, in plain English. If a page uses a word that isn't here, tell me and I'll add it.

Agent — an AI that can do things (read files, write drafts, run steps), not just chat.

Automation — a job set up to run without you starting it each time.

Commit — in Git, a saved snapshot of your project with a note about what changed.

Context / context window — everything the AI can see right now: your message, the conversation so far, attached files. If it's not in the window, the AI doesn't know it.

Folder agent — an agent that works inside one folder on your computer (Claude Code, Cursor).

Git — a tool that tracks versions of a project folder so you can save, share, and undo.

Hallucination — when AI confidently states something false. The fix is sources you can check, not trust.

LLM (large language model) — the technology behind AI chat tools. You can just say "the AI."

MCP — the standard plug that connects AI tools to apps like your calendar or files. Think "USB for AI."

Model — a specific AI (Claude, GPT, Gemini are families of models). Newer models are generally more capable.

Playbook — on this site: one useful job packaged so your AI can install it, remember it, and run it by interview. See how playbooks work.

Prompt — your request to the AI. Nothing fancier than that.

Project instructions — rules you write once that apply to every chat in a project.

Skill — the file a playbook installs into your vault so the AI can run the job later.

Subagent — a helper AI that the main AI hands part of a job to.

Terminal — the text box where you type commands to your computer.

Token — the small chunks of text AI reads and writes; pricing and limits are counted in them. You rarely need to think about tokens directly.

Tool — anything the AI can use besides talking: file access, web search, an app connection.

Vault — a folder of plain-text notes that serves as your (and your AI's) long-term memory.

Workflow — the full path a job takes: trigger → input → AI step → check → approval → delivery.

Next step: Learning links

docs.title: A rule for everything else
docs.text:
Before following any tutorial: check the date (AI advice ages in months), prefer official docs over screenshots of them, and never paste a command you can't explain — ask AI to explain it first.

Next step: Start here

docs.title: Fix It in Order
docs.text: Troubleshooting

docs.title: Fix it in order
docs.text: When something breaks, people reinstall everything, try random fixes, and make it worse. This order works for almost any computer problem — AI-related or not.

docs.title: The universal order
docs.text:
1. Read the actual error. Copy the exact message. Don't summarize it as "it's broken" — the message usually names the problem.
  2. Change nothing; try again once. Lots of failures are one-time hiccups.
  3. Ask: what changed? It worked before. What's different — an update, a new file, a rename, a different folder?
  4. Shrink the problem. Does it fail with a tiny test file? In a fresh folder? Finding where it works tells you where it breaks.
  5. Search the exact message. Paste the error, in quotes, into a search engine or an AI.
  6. Then, and only then, reinstall.

docs.title: Ask AI the right way
docs.text:
Copy this
Something failed. Here's the exact error message: [paste it].
Here's what I was doing: [one sentence]. Here's what changed
recently: [anything you know]. Walk me through fixing it one
step at a time — one step per message, and tell me what each
step does before I run it.

The "one step per message" part matters — it stops the AI from dumping ten commands you can't judge.

docs.title: By system
docs.text:
• Terminal basics — the five commands that cover 90% of it.
  • Windows · macOS — the usual suspects on each.
  • Git & Codespaces — for site and code projects.

Next step: Terminal basics

docs.title: Git & Codespaces
docs.text: Troubleshooting · Git & Codespaces

docs.title: Git & Codespaces
docs.text: You only need Git if you keep a project on GitHub — like this site. Here's the minimum that keeps you safe, in plain words.

docs.title: Git in one paragraph
docs.text: Git tracks versions of a folder. A commit is a snapshot with a note. Push sends your snapshots to GitHub. A Codespace is a computer in your browser already connected to your project — the easiest way to work on a GitHub project without installing anything.

docs.title: The daily loop
docs.text:
git status                      ← what changed?
git add -A                      ← include everything changed
git commit -m "what I did"      ← snapshot with a note
git push                        ← send it to GitHub

Run git status before and after. It's Git's "where am I?" command.

docs.title: The messages that scare people
docs.text:
• "nothing to commit" — you haven't changed anything (or already committed it). Not an error.
  • "rejected … fetch first" — GitHub has changes you don't. Run git pull, then push again.
  • "merge conflict" — the same lines changed in two places, and Git wants a human to pick. Open the file, look for <<<<<<< markers, keep the version you want, delete the markers, then add/commit/push. Or paste the conflicted file into AI and say "help me resolve this conflict — I want to keep X."

docs.title: The safety rule
docs.text: Commit before you experiment. A commit is a save point; anything can be undone after one. If you're about to let an agent loose on the project, commit first, always.

docs.title: Undo, the safe way
docs.text:
Made a mess since your last commit?

git restore .        ← put every file back to the last commit

That's the beginner-safe undo. For anything scarier (undoing pushed commits), ask AI to walk you through it one step at a time rather than pasting internet commands.

Next step: Your first agent

docs.title: macOS
docs.text: Troubleshooting · macOS

docs.title: macOS
docs.text: The usual suspects on a Mac, and the plain fix for each.

docs.title: "App can't be opened" / unidentified developer
docs.text: macOS blocks apps it doesn't recognize. If it came from the tool's official site: System Settings → Privacy & Security → scroll down → Open Anyway. Not sure it's official? Stop and check first.

docs.title: It wants permission for folders
docs.text: The first time a terminal or agent touches Documents/Desktop, macOS asks. This is good — it's your least-privilege system working. Allow the specific folder your practice work lives in; don't grant Full Disk Access just to make dialogs go away.

docs.title: "command not found" after installing
docs.text:
Same story as every system: the terminal doesn't know where the new program lives.

  1. Fully quit Terminal (Cmd+Q) and reopen. Try again.
  2. Still failing? Ask AI: "I installed [tool] on macOS and get 'command not found'. Walk me through fixing my PATH one step at a time."

docs.title: Homebrew, in one paragraph
docs.text: Guides will tell you to "brew install" things. Homebrew is a free installer for terminal tools — one command installs it (get it from brew.sh, the official site), and afterwards brew install [tool] handles the fiddly parts. It's safe and standard; just get the install command from brew.sh itself, not a random blog.

docs.title: Where your stuff is
docs.text:
Your files live under /Users/yourname/ — the terminal shortcut for it is ~. So cd ~/Documents/ai-practice means "go to the ai-practice folder in my Documents." When a command fails with "no such file or directory," you're usually one cd away from where you meant to be.

Next step: Terminal basics

docs.title: Terminal Basics
docs.text: Troubleshooting · Terminal

docs.title: Terminal basics
docs.text: The terminal is a text box where you type commands to your computer. Agents like Claude Code live there. You need about five commands and one habit — that's it.

docs.title: Open it
docs.text:
• Windows: Start menu → type "Terminal" → open Windows Terminal (or PowerShell).
  • Mac: Cmd+Space → type "Terminal" → Enter.

docs.title: The five commands
docs.text:
Type this It means pwd "Where am I?" — shows your current folder ls (Mac) / dir (Windows) "What's in here?" — lists the folder's contents cd Documents "Go into the Documents folder" cd .. "Go up one folder" mkdir ai-practice "Make a new folder called ai-practice"

That's genuinely most of what you'll use. Everything else, you can ask AI for as you need it.

docs.title: The one habit
docs.text: Know where you are before you run anything. Type pwd first. Agents act in the folder you're standing in — being in the wrong folder is the #1 beginner mistake.

docs.title: When a command fails
docs.text:
Read the message. The classics:

  • "command not found" — a typo, or the program isn't installed.
  • "no such file or directory" — you're in the wrong folder, or the name's misspelled. pwd and ls will show you.
  • "permission denied" — the computer is protecting something. Stop and ask AI about the specific message rather than forcing it.

Windows help macOS help

Next step: Git & Codespaces

docs.title: Windows
docs.text: Troubleshooting · Windows

docs.title: Windows
docs.text: The usual suspects when AI tools misbehave on Windows, and the plain fix for each.

docs.title: "Windows protected your PC" when installing
docs.text: SmartScreen warns about unfamiliar downloads. If you downloaded from the tool's official site, click More info → Run anyway. If you're not sure it's the official site, stop and check first.

docs.title: PowerShell vs Command Prompt confusion
docs.text: They're two different text boxes with slightly different commands. When a guide's commands fail, you may be in the wrong one. Use Windows Terminal and check the tab label; guides on this site work in PowerShell.

docs.title: "Not recognized as a command" after installing
docs.text:
The program installed, but Windows doesn't know where it lives yet. Two fixes, in order:

  1. Close the terminal completely and open a new one. (Fixes it half the time.)
  2. Still failing? Ask AI: "I installed [tool] on Windows and get 'not recognized'. Walk me through checking my PATH one step at a time." PATH is just the list of places Windows looks for programs.

docs.title: Paths look different — that's fine
docs.text: Windows writes folders as C:\Users\You\Documents. Guides written for Mac use /home/you/documents. Same idea, different punctuation. When copying a command, swap the path for your real one.

docs.title: Files hiding their endings
docs.text: Windows hides .txt/.md by default, so notes.md shows as "notes." In File Explorer: View → Show → File name extensions. Turn it on and leave it on — you can't work with agents while extensions are hidden.

docs.title: Antivirus quietly blocking
docs.text:
If a tool worked yesterday and silently fails today, check your antivirus's quarantine or "protected folders" screen. Add your practice folder as an allowed location if it got flagged.

Next step: Terminal basics

docs.title: Give your agent one prompt
docs.text:
Copy the promptUse the copy button on the setup prompt below. Paste it into your agentUse Codex or Claude Code on the Windows PC where VALE will run. Let the agent verify everythingIt downloads, checks, installs, connects, tests, and starts VALE. 
Install the latest public VALE for Windows release from:
https://github.com/jordysupport/jordysupport.github.io/releases/latest

Complete and verify the setup for me. Do not ask me to run terminal commands that you can safely run yourself.

1. Confirm this is 64-bit Windows 10 or Windows 11.
2. Download VALE-Windows-x64.zip and VALE-Windows-x64.zip.sha256 from the latest release into a new temporary folder.
3. Verify the ZIP against the SHA-256 checksum. Stop if verification fails.
4. Extract it and read README.md, RELEASE_NOTES.md, and INSTALL WITH YOUR AI AGENT.md.
5. Confirm your own CLI is authenticated with my existing subscription: use `codex login status` if you are Codex or `claude auth status` if you are Claude Code. Never request, reveal, copy, or manufacture an API key or authentication token. If the official CLI is unavailable, install it from the provider's official documentation and ask me only to complete an official browser sign-in if required.
6. Run scripts\Install-VALEForAgent.ps1 with `-AgentRuntime Codex` or `-AgentRuntime Claude`, matching the agent you are. Use the default per-user install location. Do not skip integrity checks, dependencies, the response probe, or diagnostics.
7. Run the installed scripts\Test-VALEInstallation.ps1, resolve every required failure, start VALE, and report the version, selected agent runtime, diagnostic result, and remaining warnings. Never include credentials or authentication-file contents.

VALE must use my authenticated agent CLI as its language-model backend. Whisper transcription and Kokoro speech generation must remain local. Do not configure a metered API key unless I explicitly reject subscription-agent mode and request an API endpoint instead.

This reuses the agent CLI's existing subscription session. VALE does not read, copy, or store the login. Codex runs ephemerally in a read-only sandbox; Claude runs with tools disabled and session persistence off. Agent usage is still subject to the limits of the person's plan.

The first setup downloads the local Kokoro voice runtime, so the agent may need several minutes and at least 4 GB of free disk space.

Download the Windows preview SHA-256 checksum

docs.title: What it does
docs.text:
• Four native visual worlds

    ECLIPSE, AXIOM, COSMOS, and PELAGOS have their own geometry, transitions, voice states, and music behavior.

  • A real voice line

    Hold Right Ctrl to speak. Whisper transcribes locally; Kokoro replies with the Bella voice by default.

  • Music that moves the field

    Play music in Spotify Desktop. VALE reads the Windows media session and system audio so each field reacts in its own visual language.

  • Cinema without the clutter

    Full-screen display controls, screen selection, captions, and a cinema mode designed for a dedicated monitor.

docs.title: No separate API billing required
docs.text:
The recommended setup connects VALE to an already-authenticated Codex or Claude Code CLI. It does not turn a subscription login into an API key, and it never copies agent credentials. Instead, a loopback-only bridge asks the signed-in CLI for each response using the person's existing plan access.

People who prefer Ollama or another OpenAI-compatible endpoint can still use the manual Install VALE.cmd path and provide their own endpoint. An API key is needed only when that chosen endpoint requires one.

docs.title: Privacy in plain English
docs.text:
• Your microphone recording is transcribed on your PC.
  • Kokoro generates VALE's speech on your PC.
  • In agent mode, only the conversation text is handed to the authenticated local agent CLI.
  • VALE does not read or store the agent CLI's credentials.
  • In manual endpoint mode, any optional API key stays in the local VALE installation.
  • The public download contains no personal token, machine path, or private network address.

docs.title: Controls
docs.text:
Control Action Hold Right Ctrl Speak to VALE 1-4 Switch visual fields M Toggle cinema mode C Toggle captions Spotify Desktop Drive music visuals and playback controls

If setup or audio ever behaves unexpectedly, run Check VALE from the Start Menu. It verifies the download, local runtimes, agent authentication, microphone visibility, browser, and port conflicts without showing credentials.

Windows preview

This release is built and tested for 64-bit Windows. It requires Chrome or Microsoft Edge and an internet connection during the first installation.
